query: initial fluid therapy for pediatric sepsis

retrieved_docs.title: Fluid Management -- Technique or Treatment -- Infants and Children
retrieved_docs.content: Moderate hypovolemia: Infants and children with moderate hypovolemia, typically defined as approximately 7% volume depletion, may be treated with a 10 mL/kg bolus of isotonic saline without dextrose. If the patient is able to tolerate it, they may then receive an additional bolus or transition to oral rehydration. [28] Indications for continued IV therapy include an inability to tolerate oral rehydration, a caregiver's inability to administer oral fluids, persistent electrolyte disturbances requiring frequent monitoring, or ongoing fluid losses that prevent adequate hydration through oral rehydration alone. Similar to adults, colloid solutions such as albumin are not considered first-line but may be helpful in pediatric patients with nephrotic syndrome or sepsis. In these cases, the recommended dosage of albumin is 0.5 mg to 1 g/kg.

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 7: Sepsis Awareness and Education
retrieved_docs.content: A process for screening and early recognition of patients with sepsis and septic shock. A process to rapidly identify and document individuals appropriate for treatment of sepsis and septic shock, including explicit criteria defining certain patients who should be excluded from the protocols, such as patients who are receiving palliative care. Guidelines for hemodynamic support, including monitoring, therapeutic endpoints, and time frame goals For infants and children, guidelines for fluid resuscitation with explicit time frames for vascular access and fluid delivery consistent with current, evidence-based guidelines for severe sepsis and septic shock with defined therapeutic goals for children. A procedure for identifying the source of infection and delivering early antibiotics with specific time frame goals.[N.Y. Comp. Codes R. & Regs. tit. 10, §§ 405.2 and 405.4]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Fluid Management -- Technique or Treatment -- Rate
retrieved_docs.content: Parental fluid administration includes bolus and maintenance rates. Fluid bolus should be rapidly infused at 10 to 20 mL/kg of isotonic saline (0.9%). [2] This should be infused over 20 minutes in children with moderate dehydration and as fast as possible in the presence of severe dehydration. A hypotonic fluid or dextrose-containing fluid should not be used for bolus unless the rapid correction of hypoglycemia is needed. A single 20-mL/kg bolus improves circulation but cannot normalize the hemodynamic status. Therefore, it can be repeated as needed until adequate perfusion is restored with careful monitoring of the clinical condition and vital signs. An improvement in clinical status and resolution of signs of dehydration, such as tachycardia and dry mucous membranes, can be easily monitored.  A fluid requirement of more than 60 ml/kg without improvement in clinical status indicates other causes such as septic shock or hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric and Neonatal Resuscitation -- Technique or Treatment -- Circulation
retrieved_docs.content: Isotonic crystalloids are the preferred fluids for resuscitating children in shock. An initial bolus of 20 mL/kg is recommended for signs of shock, with repeated boluses if systemic perfusion does not improve. Resuscitation for hypovolemia or sepsis may require 40 to 60 mL/kg. Children who remain in shock after 60 mL/kg of intravenous fluids generally require initiation of vasopressor therapy (see Image. Medications Used in Neonatal Resuscitation and Pediatric Advanced Life Support. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Fluid Management -- Technique or Treatment -- Infants and Children
retrieved_docs.content: Severe hypovolemia: In pediatric patients, severe hypovolemia is typically defined as a volume depletion of 10% or more. Initial management involves administering a 20 mL/kg bolus of isotonic saline without dextrose, followed by clinical reassessment. Boluses may be repeated as needed until the patient’s volume status stabilizes. After emergent volume repletion, subsequent fluid selection should be guided by the patient’s serum sodium concentration. Clinicians may continue isotonic saline at doses of 20 to 40 mL/kg over 2 to 4 hours. Once the patient achieves euvolemia, therapy transitions to standard maintenance fluid rates.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Sepsis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Volume Resuscitation, in the acute phase, is recommended as being 30 ml/kg of crystalloid fluids. This fixed number was taken as the average volume of fluid given to patients in the PROCESS [23] , ARISE [24] , and PROMISE [25] trials. The volume of intravenous fluids to give in early resuscitation is still a point of contention and debate, as half of those patients in the study did not receive 30 ml/kg, and half of those patients required more. The ideal fluid to administer is also a contested point, with a general recommendation for "crystalloid fluids" such as normal saline, Ringer's lactate, or balanced crystalloid solutions from the most recent Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines. [8] Recent large trials and reviews on the subject report some benefits with buffered crystalloid solutions (Ringer’s lactate) over normal saline. [29] [30] The guidelines for treating and managing sepsis do not reflect these findings.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Fluid Management -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of severe dehydration and children in shock should be given IV fluids due to the need for rapid restoration of intravascular volume. [9] This will restore adequate tissue perfusion. Children with respiratory distress should also receive IV fluids. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric and Neonatal Resuscitation -- Technique or Treatment -- Pediatric Resuscitation Recommendations and Recent Amendments
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid resuscitation for septic shock should be guided by patient response and frequent reassessment. Balanced crystalloids, unbalanced crystalloids, and colloids are all acceptable, and vasopressors such as epinephrine or norepinephrine are indicated for fluid-refractory cases. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: New York State Infection Control -- Issues of Concern -- Element 7: Sepsis Awareness and Education
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare providers should initiate fluid resuscitation with at least 30 mL/kg of intravenous crystalloid fluid as soon as possible after recognition of shock or sepsis shock, even if shock is only suspected. [59] A retrospective cohort study of adults treated for severe sepsis or septic shock in an emergency department found that failure to receive 30 mL/kg of crystalloid fluid within 3 hours of presentation was associated with higher mortality rates and longer lengths of ICU stay. [61] Continued fluid administration after the initial bolus should be guided by a careful assessment of organ perfusion and intravascular volume to balance resuscitation needs with the risks of fluid accumulation. [59] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Bacterial Sepsis ," for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: Crystalloid Fluids -- Administration -- Fluid Resuscitation
retrieved_docs.content: In 2012, the Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines recommended Early Goal-directed Therapy (EGDT) as the standard of care for managing septic shock patients. These guidelines previously required that patients receive empirically dosed rapid volume resuscitation. Patients should receive a fluid challenge of 20 mL/kg over the first 30 minutes of treatment. Subsequent volume dosing should depend on the severity of hypovolemia and should be adjusted in increments of 500 mL, aiming for an ultimate central venous pressure of 8 to 12 mm Hg. [2] However, revised guidelines in 2018 have called into question the efficacy of EGDT. The revised guidelines now state that fluid provision should be in a 1-hour bundle by administering 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate 4 mmol/L. This guideline has a "strong recommendation" rating.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypovolemia and Hypovolemic Shock -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Once the vital signs are stable, clinicians can switch to administering maintenance fluids plus enough fluids to cover any continued losses. Please see StatPearls' companion topic, " Fluid Management ," for a detailed description of calculating maintenance fluid requirements for adult and pediatric patients.  A fluid change may be necessary based on the patient's electrolyte levels, blood glucose levels, clinical condition, and renal function. If maintenance fluids are not required, then fluids are discontinued.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Fluid Management -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration due to diarrhea mainly occurs due to the contraction of intravascular fluid volume while maintaining intracellular volume. However, severe dehydration presents with early signs of hypovolemic shock. Dehydration is usually expressed as a percent of body weight loss. However, baseline hydrated weights are rarely available in the emergency department. [1] Clinically, the degree of dehydration is often divided into the following; Mild 5% Moderate 10% Severe >15% Minimal dehydration is defined as a loss of less than 3% of body weight.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluid Management -- Technique or Treatment -- Infants and Children
retrieved_docs.content: Mild-to-moderate hypovolemia: Ideally, children with mild to moderate hypovolemia should receive oral rehydration therapy. Caregivers provide frequent, small amounts of fluid through a syringe or spoon.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Fluid Management -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Administration of fluid resuscitation is essential in critically ill children. Fluid management is critical when providing acute care in the emergency department or hospitalized children. Early and appropriate fluid administration improves outcomes and reduces mortality in children. Water is essential for cellular homeostasis. There are two major fluid compartments: the intracellular fluid (ICF) and the extracellular fluid (ECF). Two-thirds of the total body water (TBW) is intracellular. The TBW varies with age; 70% in infants, 65% in children, and 60% in adults. The human body has strict physiologic control to maintain a balance of fluid and electrolytes. However, in disease states, these mechanisms may be overwhelmed. Dehydration occurs due to the significant depletion of water and electrolytes. It commonly increases morbidity and mortality in children. Infants and young children are quite sensitive to even a small degree of dehydration. This may be due to:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 306
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone acetate, a corticosteroid formulation, may be employed in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). By providing anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, dexamethasone acetate can help modulate the excessive systemic inflammatory response that characterizes septic shock, potentially improving hemodynamic stability and patient outcomes when used according to appropriate clinical guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisol succinate is identified as a potential therapeutic agent for the management of shock. Shock, a life‑threatening circulatory failure, is classified in medical terminology as “صدمة إنتانيّة” (infectious shock) and also as “صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ” (cardiogenic shock), reflecting its distinct etiologic subtypes. Recognizing that cortisol succinate may be used to treat these severe shock states underscores its relevance in acute care settings where rapid reversal of hemodynamic collapse is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1962
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylefrin and dexamethasone acetate are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for the management of صدمة إنتانيّة (septic shock). Fenylefrin may be employed to treat septic shock, while dexamethasone acetate is also considered a possible treatment option for this severe systemic infection. Their use reflects an approach to address the hemodynamic instability and inflammatory response characteristic of septic shock, highlighting the relevance of these drugs in critical care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2237
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions constitute a specific category of intravenous (IV) solutions that are administered parenterally to deliver fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. As a subclass of IV solutions used for parenteral administration, these products are formulated to replace or augment blood components, provide rapid volume expansion, and supply essential nutrients when oral intake is not possible. They play a critical role in clinical settings such as fluid resuscitation for hypovolemia, management of blood loss, and provision of nutritional support for patients who require intravenous therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2133
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylefrin, a potent α‑adrenergic agonist commonly used as a vasopressor, may be employed in the treatment of صدمة إنتانيّة (septic shock). By inducing peripheral vasoconstriction, fenylefrin can help raise systemic vascular resistance and improve arterial blood pressure in patients whose circulatory collapse is driven by overwhelming infection. Its use in septic shock is typically considered when rapid restoration of hemodynamic stability is required, often in conjunction with fluid resuscitation and other supportive measures. Proper dosing and monitoring are essential to balance the benefits of increased perfusion pressure against the risk of excessive vasoconstriction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: Shock is classified as صدمة إنتانيّة, indicating that the condition known as shock falls under the category of septic shock.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 204
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a hierarchy of intravenous and irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, and related pharmacologic agents, emphasizing their therapeutic indications, contraindications, and connections to thromboembolic and shock states. CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES is classified within Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance and is used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients; it is also grouped with antidiarrheals and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents, reflecting combined electrolyte replacement and gastrointestinal therapeutic roles. BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS is a high‑level category that includes irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, and perfusion products, serving as the parent class for both “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion” and “IV solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients,” and it subsumes the broader group blood and related products. Within this

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisol succinate, a synthetic glucocorticoid preparation, may be employed in the treatment of shock, where its rapid anti‑inflammatory and vasopressor effects can help stabilize hemodynamics and support organ perfusion in critically ill patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1852
retrieved_docs.content: Solutions producing osmotic diuresis are a specific type of IV solution used in the parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. By creating an osmotic gradient that draws water into the renal tubules, these solutions promote diuresis, helping to manage fluid balance, correct electrolyte disturbances, and support nutritional needs in patients requiring intravenous therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: irrigating solutions and electrolyte‑balancing formulations are organized within a unified pharmacological framework, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate intravenous therapies for fluid resuscitation, nutrition, electrolyte management, antifibrinolytic treatment and metabolic modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2246
retrieved_docs.content: Tromethamin is categorized within the therapeutic class of Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders, indicating that it functions as a volume‑expanding agent used to restore or maintain intravascular fluid volume. This classification reflects its role in clinical situations such as hemorrhage, hypovolemia, or other conditions requiring rapid hemodynamic support, where augmenting plasma volume is essential. Recognizing tromethamin as part of the Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders class helps clinicians select appropriate volume‑expanding therapies and understand its mechanism of modifying blood components to increase circulatory volume.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: upper GI hemorrhage. Collectively, these interrelated entities demonstrate that disturbances in fluid volume, electrolyte concentrations, acid‑base balance, and glucose homeostasis are central to the pathophysiology of acute renal failure, pancreatitis, hepatic injury, urinary infections, respiratory disease, and gastrointestinal bleeding, guiding clinicians toward comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of patients with overlapping metabolic and organ‑specific abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: serve as central nodes connecting classifications, broader disease groups, and specific clinical conditions—such as extreme preterm birth, respiratory distress, cerebral injury, fetal malnutrition, intracerebral hemorrhage, and perinatal infection—highlighting their relevance for assessment, monitoring, and management of perinatal and neonatal patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 604
retrieved_docs.content: Enteral and parenteral nutrition is a therapeutic domain that includes procedures delivering nutrients or fluids either through the gastrointestinal tract or directly into the bloodstream. Within this domain, Enteral infusion of nutritional substances is identified as a primary form of enteral and parenteral nutrition and is also classified as a type of Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substance. Nonoperative intubation and irrigation is likewise placed under the parent category of enteral and parenteral nutrition. The broader parent of Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substance is Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion, indicating that these nutritional infusions are regarded as therapeutic injections or perfusions. Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is another child of Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion, highlighting the shared modality of injection‑based delivery. The term enteral and parenteral nutrition is also linked to the Arabic phrase تغذية بالحقن (“nutrition by injection”), reinforcing its definition as injection‑based nutritional support. This integrated framework clarifies how various infusion‑based procedures, both nutritional and immunologic, are organized under common therapeutic intervention categories, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate delivery methods for patient care.
